takeaway: Wikipedia defines venture capital as "a form of private equity financing provided by firms or funds to startup, early-stage, and emerging companies," typically ones "based on an innovative technology or business model" from high-technology industries. It is high-risk by nature: "Because startups face high uncertainty, VC investments have high rates of failure."

Wikipedia defines an angel investor as "an individual who provides capital to a business or businesses, including startups, usually in exchange for convertible debt or ownership equity." Angels differ from venture capitalists because they "typically invest their funds (unlike venture capitalists, who manage the pooled money of others in a professionally managed fund)" and "often provide support to startups at a very early stage," along with "feedback, advice, and contacts."

Hustle Fund explains the power law: "Power law in startup investing means that a teeny tiny percentage of companies will deliver massive returns, and most others will return little or nothing." It calls this "the fundamental economic model that makes venture capital work" — "if just one of your companies is a huge success (and I mean huge), that will more than make up for all those other losses," illustrated by a $5,000 seed stake in Uber becoming $25 million (a 5,000x return).

Wikipedia defines carried interest ("carry") as "a share of the profits of an investment paid to the investment manager," historically "20% in private equity buyout and venture investments." The manager only collects it after investors clear a preferred return: "the customary hurdle rate is 7% to 9% per annum," the minimum return investors must receive first.

Qubit Capital describes the "2 and 20" model: the "Two" is "the annual management fee, set at 2% of committed capital, paid regardless of fund performance to cover operations," while the "Twenty" is "carried interest: the GP's 20% share of profits once returns clear the hurdle rate." In its example the general partner (GP) commits $1M of their own capital and limited partners (LPs) contribute $99M; management fees "cover salaries, rent, and deal sourcing."

Wikipedia defines seed money as "the earliest financial support a new project receives, often arriving at a point when an idea exists but nothing concrete has been built yet." Providers include "friends and family funding, seed venture capital funds, angel funding, and crowdfunding," and the capital "can be used to pay for preliminary operations such as market research and product development."

Silicon Valley Bank maps the stages by use of capital: seed funds "market research," "business plan development," and "product development"; Series A is to "fine-tune your product or service" and "expand your workforce"; Series B is to "grow your operations," "meet customer demands," and "expand to new markets"; and Series C and beyond helps businesses "build new products, reach new markets and even acquire other startups."

Visible.vc puts numbers to the stages: a pre-seed round "is generally the first round of institutional capital that a startup raises," typically $100,000 to $5M; Series A averaged $18.7 million as of 2024, with investors "typically purchase 10-30% of the company"; Series B averages $30M or more; and Series C runs $30–$100M with valuations "typically exceeding $100M."

Wikipedia defines pre-money valuation as "the valuation of a company or asset prior to an investment or financing," and notes that after the round "the company will have a valuation after the investment that is equal to the pre-money valuation plus the cash amount." The relationship is simply Pre-money valuation = Post-money valuation − Investment amount.

CRV defines equity dilution as "the reduction in your ownership percentage that occurs when your company issues new shares, even though your absolute share count stays the same." Its worked example: founders holding 8,000,000 of 10,000,000 shares (80%) drop to 8,000,000 of 12,500,000 (64%) after a 2,500,000-share Series A. It warns that an option pool created before a round means "founders bear the cost while incoming investors remain unaffected."

EQT defines capitalization tables ("cap tables") as "an essential tool for understanding and managing company equity, financial planning, and fundraising" that "record a company's ownership structure, detailing who owns what stake and its value." Founders "maintain and update cap tables to inform planning for future funding and equity allocation and manage potential dilution."

Wall Street Prep defines a SAFE — "Simple Agreement for Future Equity" — as "a form of early-stage startup financing introduced by Y Combinator in 2013," with "no interest or maturity date," unlike convertible notes. The valuation cap "puts a maximum price tag on the company" for conversion, and the discount rate "gives the SAFE note investor a discounted price per share compared to the price paid by new investors" — e.g. "a 20% discount means the SAFE investor gets shares at 80% of the new round's price."

Cake Equity explains SAFE mechanics: "the valuation cap sets the maximum valuation at which the investor's investment will convert into equity," pricing conversion "at whichever is lower: the specified cap or the company's valuation in the subsequent funding round." The discount "allows the investor's investment to convert into equity at a discounted price compared to the price paid by new investors," and when a priced round occurs "the SAFE note holders will then become shareholders."

The Startup Law Blog defines a convertible note as "a short-term debt instrument that converts into equity — typically preferred stock — when a startup raises a priced funding round," carrying "interest accruing at 4–8% annually, a discount rate on future share prices, and a valuation cap." The key difference: "a convertible note is debt — it accrues interest and has a maturity date — while a SAFE is not debt, has no interest or maturity, and only converts on a triggering event."

Wikipedia defines a term sheet as "a bullet-point document outlining the material terms and conditions of a potential business agreement, establishing the basis for future negotiations." Term sheets "may be either binding or non-binding," and once signed a term sheet "guides legal counsel in the preparation of a proposed 'definitive agreement'" — a preliminary blueprint before attorneys draft the full legal documents.

Wikipedia describes a liquidation preference as "one of the primary economic terms of a venture finance investment" that "establishes that certain investors receive their investment money back first before other company owners" in a sale or wind-down. A participating preference means investors "receive their preference first and are then entitled to a share of any remaining funds," while non-participating means they take "either their preference amount only... or else their proportionate share of distributions... whichever is greater."

Forum Ventures lists the four criteria VCs weigh in early-stage startups: "Team – The founder's lived experience, grit, and resilience"; "Product – Proof that your solution solves a real problem"; "Market – A scalable market opportunity with room for growth"; and "Traction – Signals of momentum, whether it's revenue, customer feedback, or product adoption." It stresses that "at the earliest stages, the founding team is the single most important factor in an investment decision."
Aswath Damodaran gives a 12-minute overview of the three core financial statements — income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow — as the foundation for valuation and financial analysis.
Aswath Damodaran's 18-minute lecture (Session 6) explaining key financial ratios used to assess company performance, profitability, leverage, and efficiency.
15-minute lecture by Aswath Damodaran on currency risk in valuation, part of his corporate finance or valuation course series, explaining how to handle multi-currency cash flows.
Discounted cash flow (DCF) is a valuation method used to estimate the attractiveness of an investment opportunity. Learn how it is calculated and when to use it.
Session 3 of Aswath Damodaran's finance course covering balance sheet structure, components, and interpretation.
